questions.q: A project will replace a payment platform. The regulatory core has fixed, well-understood requirements, but the customer-facing portal has rapidly evolving needs. What development approach should the PM recommend?
opts: Fully predictive, since payments are regulated
Fully agile, since some requirements are evolving
A hybrid approach — predictive for the stable regulated core, agile for the evolving portal
Whichever approach the PMO template prescribes
questions.exp: The 2026 ECO expects the PM to assess needs, complexity, and magnitude and recommend predictive, adaptive, or hybrid accordingly. Stable, compliance-driven scope suits predictive control; evolving scope suits iterative delivery. Hybrid applies each where it fits.

questions.q: While consolidating subsidiary plans into the integrated project management plan, the PM notices the schedule baseline assumes two testers that the resource management plan never provides. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Baseline the plan and raise a risk for the gap
Reconcile the dependency and resolve the gap across the subsidiary plans before baselining
Ask the sponsor for two additional testers
Remove the testing activities from the schedule
questions.exp: Assessing consolidated plans for dependencies, gaps, and continued business value is an explicit enabler of developing the integrated PM plan. Baselining a plan with a known internal contradiction institutionalizes the error — reconcile first.

questions.q: A new team member says, 'We're agile — why do we have a plan at all?' What is the BEST response from the agile practitioner leading the effort?
opts: Agile teams do not plan; they respond to change
Agile planning is continuous — vision, release, iteration, and daily levels — rather than a single upfront plan
Only the Product Owner is allowed to plan
Planning is done once at project kickoff and frozen
questions.exp: Agile is not plan-free; it plans at multiple horizons (product vision, roadmap/release, iteration, daily) and progressively elaborates. 'Responding to change over following a plan' still values planning — it values adaptation more.

questions.q: During planning, a stakeholder notes the organization must report carbon impact for all major initiatives. The project's data needs have not yet been defined. What should the PM do?
opts: Defer sustainability data until execution when actuals exist
Identify sustainability reporting as a critical information requirement and plan how the data will be captured
Exclude it because it is a corporate reporting obligation, not project scope
Ask the PMO to handle all sustainability reporting
questions.exp: Determining critical information requirements — the ECO explicitly names sustainability as an example — is part of developing the integrated plan. If the data is never planned for, it cannot be captured later.

questions.q: A change to add a deliverable was approved by the CCB. The PM updates the scope baseline. What ELSE must the PM do?
opts: Nothing — the scope baseline is the only affected artifact
Update all affected subsidiary plans and baselines (schedule, cost, resources, risk) so the integrated plan stays consistent
Ask the sponsor to reissue the project charter
Wait until the next phase gate to update other plans
questions.exp: Maintaining the integrated project management plan means changes ripple through every affected component. A scope change almost always touches schedule, cost, resource, and risk planning — an integrated plan that disagrees with itself is worse than no plan.

questions.q: An executive insists the team 'just pick agile' before anyone has examined the project. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Adopt agile as directed — the executive has authority
Assess the project's needs, complexity, uncertainty, and constraints before recommending an approach
Run the project predictively and rebrand ceremonies with agile names
Survey the team for their preferred methodology
questions.exp: The delivery approach should follow from an assessment of the project context — requirements stability, stakeholder availability, risk, compliance, team capability — not from preference or fashion. The PM analyzes first, then recommends with rationale.

questions.q: Two workstream leads submit status data that contradict each other about the same integration milestone. The steering committee meets tomorrow. What should the PM do?
opts: Report the more optimistic version to keep the committee confident
Investigate and reconcile the data with both leads before reporting
Report both versions and let the committee decide
Skip the milestone in tomorrow's report
questions.exp: Collecting and analyzing data to make informed decisions requires reconciling conflicting inputs at the source. Reporting unverified or cherry-picked data violates both good practice and PMI's honesty standard.

questions.q: During scope definition, marketing and operations fundamentally disagree about whether customer migration is inside the project boundary. What should the PM do?
opts: Document both positions and start work on the areas of agreement
Facilitate a working session to resolve the boundary and obtain documented stakeholder agreement on scope
Let the sponsor decide unilaterally and inform both groups
Include migration to be safe and remove it later if needed
questions.exp: Obtaining stakeholder agreement on project scope is an explicit ECO enabler. Starting work with a contested boundary guarantees rework and conflict later. The PM facilitates alignment and captures the agreement in the scope statement.

questions.q: An agile team receives a large initiative described only as 'modernize the customer onboarding experience.' How should this scope be broken down?
opts: Create a complete WBS and WBS dictionary before the first sprint
Decompose it progressively — epic to features to user stories — during backlog refinement with the Product Owner
Ask the sponsor for a detailed requirements specification
Estimate the whole initiative as one story
questions.exp: Agile breaks scope down progressively: large epics are split into features and then into sprint-sized stories as they approach implementation. Refinement is ongoing and collaborative, not a one-time upfront specification exercise.

questions.q: A team keeps decomposing the WBS until every work package is a two-hour task, and planning is consuming the schedule. What guidance applies?
opts: Decompose until each package equals exactly one day of work
Stop decomposing once work can be reliably estimated and managed — excessive decomposition wastes effort
Decompose to the level the sponsor requests
The WBS should only ever have two levels
questions.exp: Decomposition stops when the level of detail supports reliable estimating and control (heuristics like the 8/80 rule). Over-decomposition adds administrative burden without improving management — a form of planning waste.

questions.q: A hybrid project has a fixed-price hardware contract with locked specifications and an in-house software team using Scrum. How should scope be managed?
opts: Manage everything through the product backlog to stay agile
Manage everything through a single scope baseline to stay controlled
Use a scope baseline with formal change control for the contracted hardware and a prioritized backlog for the software
Freeze software scope to match the hardware contract
questions.exp: Hybrid means applying the right control to each component. Contractual, fixed scope needs a baseline and integrated change control; evolving software scope is managed through backlog prioritization. Forcing one model onto both creates either rigidity or contract risk.

questions.q: A stakeholder insists a reporting feature was 'always understood to be included,' but it appears nowhere in the product backlog. What should happen?
opts: The team adds it to the current sprint to preserve the relationship
The request goes to the Product Owner, who evaluates it against the product goal and prioritizes the backlog accordingly
The Scrum Master rejects it because it is not written down
The stakeholder escalates directly to the development team
questions.exp: In agile, the backlog is the single source of scope truth and the PO owns its ordering. New or 'assumed' requirements are welcomed as backlog candidates — they are not inserted into a sprint through side channels.

questions.q: Deliverables have passed internal quality control inspections. What is the NEXT step for these deliverables?
opts: Archive them and close the work packages
Present them for scope validation — formal acceptance by the customer or sponsor
Begin warranty support
Update the WBS dictionary
questions.exp: Control Quality checks correctness internally; Validate Scope then obtains formal acceptance from the customer. The sequence matters on the exam: verify the deliverable is right, then get it formally accepted.

questions.q: At project kickoff, stakeholders each describe 'success' differently — revenue growth, reduced call volume, regulatory approval. What should the PM do FIRST to enable value-based delivery?
opts: Adopt revenue growth since it is easiest to measure
Facilitate identification of the value components with key stakeholders and document agreed value measures
Deliver the scope and let each stakeholder judge value afterwards
Ask the PMO to define success centrally
questions.exp: 'Identify value components with key stakeholders' is the first enabler of the new value-based delivery task. If value is undefined or contested, prioritization, measurement, and delivery decisions have no foundation.

questions.q: A Product Owner keeps ordering the backlog around whichever executive complained most recently. Delivered features are seeing little use. What should the agile practitioner coach the PO to do?
opts: Continue — executives are the most important stakeholders
Prioritize using value evidence such as expected benefit, cost of delay, and customer feedback rather than volume of the request
Alternate priorities between executives to be fair
Let the development team choose what to build
questions.exp: Value-based prioritization uses evidence — expected business value, cost of delay, risk reduction, customer feedback — not stakeholder loudness. Low adoption of delivered features is a signal that ordering is not tracking real value.

questions.q: A new logistics system can be deployed as a single national go-live or as a one-region pilot followed by staged rollout. The sponsor asks which option is better for demonstrating value. What should the PM do?
opts: Choose national go-live — it delivers all the value at once
Compare the delivery options against time-to-value, risk, and validated learning, and recommend based on that analysis
Choose the pilot because pilots are always safer
Let the vendor decide, since they perform the deployment
questions.exp: 'Evaluate delivery options to demonstrate value' means analyzing alternatives economically: a pilot trades total speed for earlier feedback, lower risk, and proof of value; big-bang trades risk for speed. The PM presents evidence-based options, not reflexive preferences.

questions.q: Three sprints after a major feature shipped, product analytics show almost no customers use it, while support tickets reveal an unmet need elsewhere. What should the team do?
opts: Continue the roadmap as planned — the feature was approved
Inspect the feedback with the Product Owner and reprioritize the backlog toward the demonstrated need
Market the unused feature harder
Increase velocity to ship the roadmap faster
questions.exp: Examining business value throughout the project means acting on outcome evidence. Output without adoption is not value. The empirical response is to inspect the data and adapt the backlog — not to defend the original plan.

questions.q: The business case promises a 20% reduction in claim processing time, but no one has recorded the current processing time. What should the PM do?
opts: Proceed — the benefit will be obvious once delivered
Verify a measurement system exists: capture the baseline metric now and define how benefits will be tracked
Change the business case to something easier to prove
Assign benefits measurement to whoever is free at closure
questions.exp: 'Verify a measurement system is in place to track benefits' is an explicit enabler. Without a baseline, the promised improvement can never be demonstrated. This must be established early, not reconstructed at closure.

questions.q: Stakeholders complain that monthly status decks don't show whether the product is actually getting better. What is the BEST way for the team to demonstrate value?
opts: Add more EVM detail to the status deck
Demonstrate the working increment and share outcome metrics at regular reviews
Send the full backlog export each month
Invite stakeholders to daily standups
questions.exp: Evaluating delivery options to demonstrate value favors direct evidence — working product demonstrations plus outcome metrics — over documentation about work. Sprint reviews exist precisely to make value inspectable.

questions.q: Stakeholders classify all 60 requirements as 'must have' for the first release, which pushes any go-live out by a year. What should the PM do to enable earlier value delivery?
opts: Accept the classification — stakeholders know their needs
Facilitate value-based prioritization with stakeholders (e.g., MoSCoW, WSJF) to identify a minimum release that delivers usable value sooner
Cut the bottom 30 requirements unilaterally
Ask the sponsor to overrule the stakeholders
questions.exp: When everything is 'must have,' nothing is prioritized. The PM facilitates an explicit value conversation — ranking by benefit, cost of delay, and dependency — so an early release can start returning value. Prioritization is facilitated with stakeholders, not imposed.

questions.q: Two features each take one sprint. Feature A loses the company $30,000 per month while undelivered; Feature B loses $8,000 per month. The sponsor prefers B because it is 'more visible.' Using value-based prioritization, what should the team do?
opts: Build B first — sponsor preference decides
Build A first — its cost of delay is nearly four times higher for the same effort
Build both in parallel by splitting the team
Delay both until the sponsor and PO agree
questions.exp: With equal duration, sequence by cost of delay: A at $30K/month outranks B at $8K/month. Value-based delivery uses economic reasoning — visibility and preference are not value measures. The PO should share this analysis with the sponsor.

questions.q: At a phase gate review of a predictive project, the deliverables are on time and on budget. What ELSE must be confirmed before authorizing the next phase?
opts: That the team is available for the next phase
That the project still has continued business justification and expected value
That all change requests were rejected
That the next phase budget is fully spent-down
questions.exp: Phase gates evaluate more than baseline performance — they reconfirm the business case. Continued funding of a project whose value has evaporated wastes organizational resources, no matter how well it performs against plan.

questions.q: A PM is estimating resource requirements for a construction project. The WBS and activity list are complete. Which technique produces the MOST reliable resource estimate?
opts: Analogous estimating from a similar past project
Bottom-up estimating from the activity-level requirements
Sponsor-directed estimating
Rule-of-thumb percentage of budget
questions.exp: Bottom-up estimating aggregates resource needs from the lowest-level activities — the most accurate (and most effortful) technique. Analogous estimating is faster but less reliable; it suits early, low-information phases.

questions.q: A specialist is assigned to three agile teams simultaneously and every team is blocked waiting for her. What should the agile practitioner do?
opts: Ask the specialist to work longer hours
Raise the constraint with management: reduce multitasking by dedicating her, cross-training others, or resequencing dependent work
Tell the teams to estimate around her availability
Escalate the specialist's performance to HR
questions.exp: Splitting a scarce person across teams maximizes utilization but destroys flow — context switching creates the bottleneck. Managing and optimizing resource availability means fixing the structural constraint, often via dedication or cross-training (T-shaping).

questions.q: The PM identifies that a niche security certification will be required for work starting in six months, and no current team member holds it. What should the PM do?
opts: Wait until the phase starts, then hire externally
Plan now — evaluate training a current member, hiring, or contracting, and build the choice into the resource plan
Descope the security work
Assume the functional manager will handle it
questions.exp: Defining and planning resources based on requirements is proactive. A six-month lead time is an asset: training or acquisition can be arranged without schedule impact. Waiting converts a manageable plan item into a crisis.

questions.q: Two projects both scheduled the same specialized testing rig for the same two weeks. Your project's need is flexible by a month; theirs is not. What should the PM do?
opts: Book the rig first and let the other project escalate
Coordinate with the other project manager and resource owner to optimize the shared allocation across both projects
Rent a second rig without approval
Escalate directly to the CEO
questions.exp: Managing and optimizing resource availability includes coordinating shared organizational resources across projects. Collaboration and transparency solve this; unilateral grabbing or premature escalation are classic PMI wrong answers.

questions.q: A new initiative arrives and leadership proposes dissolving a high-performing agile team to build a new team around the work. What should the agile practitioner advise?
opts: Agree — teams should be rebuilt for every initiative
Bring the work to the stable team where feasible — long-lived teams retain velocity, trust, and shared context
Split the team in half to seed two new teams
Outsource the initiative to avoid the question
questions.exp: 'Bring work to the team' is an agile resourcing principle: stable teams preserve established norms and predictable throughput. Re-forming teams resets Tuckman stages and sacrifices performance that took months to build.

questions.q: Scope for a data-center buildout is fully specified and stable, and the buyer wants maximum cost certainty. Which contract type is MOST appropriate?
opts: Cost Plus Fixed Fee (CPFF)
Time and Materials (T&M)
Firm Fixed Price (FFP)
Cost Plus Award Fee (CPAF)
questions.exp: FFP fits well-defined, stable scope: the price is locked and cost risk sits with the seller. Cost-reimbursable and T&M types shift risk toward the buyer and suit uncertain or evolving scope.

questions.q: An organization wants a vendor's agile team to deliver a product whose backlog will evolve every iteration. Which contracting approach BEST supports this?
opts: Firm fixed price against a frozen requirements specification
A capacity- or T&M-based agreement (with a not-to-exceed cap) that fixes the team and cadence while allowing scope to be reprioritized each iteration
Cost plus fixed fee with no cap and no reviews
A separate fixed-price contract negotiated for every user story
questions.exp: Agile-friendly contracts fix capacity and cadence rather than scope, letting the backlog evolve while an NTE cap controls buyer exposure. Freezing requirements in an FFP contradicts the reason agile was chosen.

questions.q: A vendor has missed its contractual service levels for two consecutive months. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Terminate the contract for cause
Review the agreement, document the performance gap, and address it with the vendor through the contract's governance mechanism
Withhold all payments immediately
Publicly re-tender the work
questions.exp: Evaluating vendor performance and managing suppliers starts with facts and the contract: document the shortfall, follow the agreed remediation/escalation path, and give the vendor the contractual opportunity to cure. Termination is a last resort with legal consequences.

questions.q: Before entering negotiations with a strategic supplier, what should the PM's negotiation preparation include?
opts: An opening demand and a refusal to move from it
Clear objectives, priorities, concession limits, and a best alternative (BATNA) — aiming for a workable agreement for both parties
Whatever terms the supplier proposes, to save time
A plan to conceal the project's real requirements
questions.exp: Determining a negotiation strategy means knowing your objectives, your walk-away point, and your alternatives before the table. PMI favors principled, win-win negotiation — durable supplier relationships outlast any single deal.

questions.q: A vendor announces its work is finished and submits a final invoice. Before closing the agreement, what must the PM verify?
opts: That the invoice matches the purchase order total
That the objectives of the procurement agreement were actually met — deliverables verified against the contracted acceptance criteria
That the vendor is willing to sign a new contract
That the warranty period has already expired
questions.exp: 'Verify objectives of the procurement agreement are met' precedes contract closure: formally confirm deliverables against contractual acceptance criteria, resolve open claims, and document. Payment follows verification, not announcement.

questions.q: A tool can be leased for $5,000 per month, or purchased for $60,000 plus $1,000 per month maintenance. After how many months of use does purchasing become the cheaper option?
opts: 12 months
15 months
20 months
24 months
questions.exp: Set lease cost equal to buy cost: 5,000m = 60,000 + 1,000m → 4,000m = 60,000 → m = 15. Beyond 15 months of expected use, buying is cheaper — the standard make-or-buy break-even analysis.

questions.q: The organization has committed to net-zero targets. The project is preparing an RFP for a major materials supplier. How should sustainability be handled?
opts: Mention it verbally to preferred bidders during Q&A
Build sustainability requirements into the RFP and weight them in the documented source selection criteria applied to all bidders
Award on lowest price and negotiate sustainability afterwards
Exclude it — procurement decisions are purely commercial
questions.exp: Sustainability commitments become procurement requirements and weighted selection criteria, defined before evaluation and applied consistently and fairly to every bidder. Retrofitting them after award weakens both compliance and negotiating position.

questions.q: A risk in the register materializes and the planned response costs $18,000, exactly as analyzed. Which funds should the PM use, and what approval is needed?
opts: Management reserve, with sponsor approval
Contingency reserve, which the PM controls for identified risks
The cost baseline's unspent activity budget, silently
A new change request to increase the project budget
questions.exp: Contingency reserve exists for identified risks (known unknowns) and is inside the cost baseline under PM control. Management reserve is for unidentified risks (unknown unknowns) and requires sponsor/governance approval to access.

questions.q: An event no one identified or could reasonably have foreseen forces $50,000 of unplanned work. Contingency reserve is fully allocated to other identified risks. What should the PM do?
opts: Take the money from contingency anyway — it exists for surprises
Request access to management reserve through the sponsor and governance process, updating budgets and documentation accordingly
Absorb the cost by cutting quality activities
Report the project over budget and do nothing
questions.exp: Unknown unknowns are what management reserve is for. It sits outside the cost baseline, so drawing on it requires sponsor/governance approval and a documented budget update — reserves are managed, not raided.

questions.q: Quantitative analysis identifies three threats: 20% probability of a $40,000 impact, 50% of $10,000, and 10% of $100,000. Using EMV, what contingency reserve should be set for these risks?
questions.exp: EMV per risk: 0.20×40,000 = 8,000; 0.50×10,000 = 5,000; 0.10×100,000 = 10,000. Sum = $23,000. Quantifying risk-based financial allocations this way is an explicit enabler of the new Plan and Manage Finance task.

questions.q: The sponsor wants forecast-versus-budget monthly, corporate finance needs actuals by cost center, and the steering committee only wants threshold breaches. How should the PM handle project financial reporting?
opts: Send everyone the same detailed spreadsheet to be consistent
Plan financial reporting deliberately — define content, format, and frequency per audience in the cost/financial management plan
Report only to the sponsor, who can forward as needed
Wait for each group to request reports individually
questions.exp: 'Plan financial reporting' is an explicit enabler of the 2026 finance task: different governance audiences legitimately need different financial views and cadences. Designing this once, upfront, prevents both information gaps and reporting churn.

questions.q: An agile team costs $60,000 per sprint and the remaining approved funding is $420,000. The Product Owner asks what this means for planning. What is the BEST answer?
opts: Funding is irrelevant to backlog ordering
At the current burn rate the team has about 7 sprints of runway, so the backlog should be ordered to deliver the highest value within that horizon
The team should slow its burn rate by working fewer hours
Runway calculations only apply to predictive projects
questions.exp: 420,000 ÷ 60,000 = 7 sprints of runway. In agile funding models, a stable team cost makes finance simple and powerful: the honest question becomes 'what is the most valuable use of 7 sprints?' — which is exactly what value-based backlog ordering answers.

questions.q: Cost variance breaches the ±8% threshold defined in the project's governance framework. What should the PM do?
opts: Wait one more period to see if the variance self-corrects
Analyze the cause, prepare a forecast and response options, and engage the governance process as the threshold requires
Move budget between control accounts to mask the variance
Lower the threshold to ±15% so no breach exists
questions.exp: 'Monitor financial variations and work with the governance process' is an explicit enabler. Thresholds exist to trigger escalation with analysis and options — hiding, reclassifying, or redefining the variance are integrity failures.

questions.q: A three-year project will buy specialized components from an overseas supplier in years two and three. Inflation and currency movement are concerns. What should the PM do during financial planning?
opts: Ignore it — future prices are unknowable
Anticipate the exposure: consider economic price adjustment terms, currency provisions, and appropriate financial reserves for the risk
Buy all three years of components immediately regardless of storage cost or cash flow
Plan using today's prices and raise change requests when prices move
questions.exp: 'Anticipate future finance challenges' means treating inflation, exchange rates, and market volatility as plannable financial risks — through contract mechanisms (e.g., FP-EPA), reserves, and timing strategies — not as future surprises.

questions.q: The cost baseline is $2,000,000 and management reserve is $150,000. A stakeholder asks for the total project budget. What is it, and can the PM spend the reserve on identified risks?
opts: $2,000,000 — management reserve is not part of the budget
$2,150,000 — and no, management reserve requires governance approval and is not for identified risks
$2,150,000 — and yes, the PM may spend it on any risk
$1,850,000 — reserves are subtracted from the baseline
questions.exp: Project budget = cost baseline + management reserve = $2,150,000. Contingency reserve (inside the baseline) covers identified risks under PM control; management reserve covers unknown unknowns and needs sponsor/governance approval.

questions.q: Halfway through the schedule, 80% of the contingency reserve has been consumed while the risk register still carries most of its original exposure. What should the PM do?
opts: Nothing — reserves exist to be spent
Reassess remaining risk exposure against remaining reserve, and raise the projected shortfall with the sponsor and governance now
Quietly reclassify future risk costs as scope changes
Stop implementing risk responses to preserve the reserve
questions.exp: Managing financial reserves means continuously comparing reserve remaining to risk exposure remaining. A projected shortfall is material governance information — surfacing it early preserves options; concealing it converts a finance problem into an ethics problem.

questions.q: A sponsor demands a precise month-by-month cost forecast for the next 18 months from a Scrum team whose backlog beyond one quarter is intentionally coarse. What should the PM provide?
opts: A single-point forecast to satisfy the request, adjusted later
A forecast built from stable team cost per sprint and the product roadmap, presented with confidence ranges that widen over the horizon
A refusal — agile teams do not forecast
The previous project's forecast with updated dates
questions.exp: Agile financial forecasting is empirical: team run-rate × planned sprints gives a reliable cost curve, while scope confidence decreases with distance. Presenting ranges is honest and useful; false single-point precision is neither.

questions.q: A manufacturer is categorizing quality costs. Which of the following is an EXTERNAL failure cost?
opts: Training assemblers in the new soldering standard
Inspecting units before they leave the factory
Scrapping defective units found on the production line
Recalling and repairing units that failed at customer sites
questions.exp: Cost of Quality: prevention (training) and appraisal (inspection) are costs of conformance; internal failure (scrap found in-house) and external failure (recalls, warranty, reputational damage after delivery) are costs of nonconformance. External failure is the most expensive category.

questions.q: A project's quality costs are: prevention $10,000, appraisal $15,000, internal rework $40,000, warranty claims $60,000. What is the cost of nonconformance?
questions.exp: Cost of nonconformance = internal failure + external failure = 40,000 + 60,000 = $100,000. Prevention and appraisal ($25,000) are costs of conformance. Spending four times more on failure than on conformance signals underinvestment in prevention.

questions.q: A quality review shows 30% of fabricated components are being scrapped. The sponsor notes the scrap rate also conflicts with the organization's published waste-reduction commitments. How should the PM treat this?
opts: As a cost issue only — sustainability is a corporate matter
As both an internal failure cost and a sustainability impact: perform root cause analysis and invest in prevention to reduce scrap at the source
As acceptable, since scrap was included in the estimates
As a procurement problem to pass to the materials vendor
questions.exp: The 2026 quality task pairs cost of quality with sustainability: scrap is simultaneously an internal failure cost and a resource-waste impact. Root cause analysis plus prevention investment reduces both — one improvement serving cost, quality, and sustainability goals.

questions.q: The same category of defect has escaped to production in three consecutive sprints. What should the team do?
opts: Add a hardening sprint every quarter
Perform root cause analysis in the retrospective and update the Definition of Done or engineering practices to prevent recurrence
Assign blame to the developer who wrote the code
Ask the Product Owner to accept the defects as known issues
questions.exp: Recurring defects are a process signal, not a person problem. Continuous improvement means finding the systemic cause and hardening the process — often via the Definition of Done, automated checks, or reviews — so the class of defect cannot escape again.

questions.q: A medical-device project must comply with a strict regulatory standard. When should compliance verification activities be planned?
opts: After development, during final inspection
During quality planning — identify the applicable standards and design verification and documentation activities into the plan
Only if the regulator schedules an audit
After the first nonconformance is found
questions.exp: 'Help ensure regulatory compliance' lives inside quality planning: identify applicable standards early and design the verification, traceability, and documentation into the work. Discovering compliance gaps at final inspection is the most expensive possible timing.

questions.q: Peer-review data shows the defect discovery rate has doubled over the last six weeks. What should the PM do FIRST?
opts: Replace the reviewers with more experienced staff
Investigate the trend to find the process cause — new components, schedule pressure, changed practices — before acting
Suspend peer reviews since they are finding too many problems
Celebrate, since more defects found means better reviews
questions.exp: Ongoing quality reviews produce trend data; a doubled defect rate demands causal investigation before intervention. Reacting without understanding (blaming people, suspending the control) is the classic wrong answer — the review is the messenger, not the problem.

questions.q: A newly formed agile team plans to rely on a two-week manual test phase at the end of each release. What should the agile practitioner coach instead?
opts: Extend the test phase to three weeks for safety
Build quality into every increment — automated testing, continuous integration, and a strong Definition of Done — rather than inspecting quality in at the end
Skip testing since the PO reviews each demo
Outsource testing to a separate quality department
questions.exp: Prevention over inspection is amplified in agile: quality is built into each increment through automated tests, CI, and the DoD. An end-of-release manual test phase recreates the waterfall pattern agile is meant to replace and delays feedback.

questions.q: A quality engineer proposes $20,000 of additional automated checks and design reviews, projecting a $75,000 lifecycle reduction in rework and warranty costs. The sponsor calls it 'gold plating.' What should the PM explain?
opts: The sponsor is right — any extra quality spend is gold plating
This is prevention investment that reduces total cost of quality; gold plating is adding unrequested features, not strengthening conformance to requirements
Quality investments never need justification
The project should cut the proposal to $5,000 as a compromise
questions.exp: Managing cost of quality means optimizing total CoQ: prevention spending that reduces larger failure costs is sound economics. Gold plating means delivering beyond requirements — investing to reliably meet requirements is the opposite of gold plating.

questions.q: A project has PV = $120,000, EV = $100,000, and AC = $110,000. What is the project's status?
opts: Under budget and ahead of schedule
Over budget and behind schedule (CV = −$10,000, SV = −$20,000)
Over budget but ahead of schedule
Under budget but behind schedule
questions.exp: CV = EV − AC = 100,000 − 110,000 = −$10,000 (over budget). SV = EV − PV = 100,000 − 120,000 = −$20,000 (behind schedule). EV comes first in both formulas; negative is always unfavorable.

questions.q: BAC = $300,000, EV = $120,000, AC = $150,000. What is the TCPI to complete the project on the original budget, and what does it mean?
opts: 0.80 — the team can relax its efficiency
1.20 — the remaining work must be performed 20% more cost-efficiently than planned, which is difficult
1.00 — the project is exactly on track
2.00 — the budget must be doubled
questions.exp: TCPI = (BAC − EV) ÷ (BAC − AC) = 180,000 ÷ 150,000 = 1.2. Current CPI is 0.8, so the team must jump from $0.80 of value per dollar to $1.20 — a warning sign that the original budget is likely unachievable.

questions.q: An activity has Early Start = day 5, Early Finish = day 9, and Late Start = day 8. What is its total float, and is it on the critical path?
opts: Float = 3 days; not on the critical path
Float = 0 days; on the critical path
Float = 4 days; not on the critical path
Cannot be determined without Late Finish
questions.exp: Total float = LS − ES = 8 − 5 = 3 days. Any positive float means the activity is not critical — it can slip up to 3 days without moving the project end date. Zero float defines the critical path.

questions.q: An activity's estimates are Optimistic = 6 days and Pessimistic = 18 days. Using PERT, what is the standard deviation of the estimate?
opts: 4 days
2 days
3 days
12 days
questions.exp: Standard deviation = (P − O) ÷ 6 = (18 − 6) ÷ 6 = 2 days. Note that Most Likely is not used for the standard deviation — only for the PERT mean, (O + 4M + P) ÷ 6.

questions.q: A release requires 240 remaining story points and the team's velocity has averaged 30 points per sprint over the last five sprints. What is the MOST defensible release forecast?
opts: Exactly 8 sprints, guaranteed
About 8 sprints, presented as a forecast with a range, revisited as velocity data accumulates
4 sprints if the team doubles its effort
The team cannot forecast without converting points to hours
questions.exp: 240 ÷ 30 = 8 sprints is the empirical forecast — but velocity is historical data, not a promise. Presenting a range and re-forecasting each sprint reflects how agile estimation actually works; treating it as a guarantee misuses the metric.

questions.q: A vendor delay pushes a hardware milestone out by three weeks. What should the PM analyze FIRST when assessing schedule impact?
opts: The vendor's penalty clauses
Whether the delayed work is on the critical path and how the slip propagates through dependent activities
Which team member can absorb overtime
How to reword the status report
questions.exp: Analyzing schedule variation starts with the network: a 3-week slip on a path with 4 weeks of float changes nothing, while the same slip on the critical path moves the end date. Impact analysis precedes response selection.

questions.q: Estimating a new office fit-out, the PM uses the duration of a nearly identical fit-out completed last year as the basis. This technique is:
opts: Bottom-up estimating — the most accurate technique
Analogous estimating — fast and useful early, but less accurate than detailed methods
Parametric estimating using a statistical model
Three-point estimating
questions.exp: Using historical data from a comparable project is analogous estimating — an ECO enabler ('utilize benchmarks and historical data'). It is quick and cheap but less accurate than parametric or bottom-up methods; accuracy improves as detail becomes available.

questions.q: Mid-sprint, a team discovers its top story cannot finish because another team's API will not be ready for two more weeks. What should happen FIRST?
opts: Quietly move the story to the next sprint and say nothing
Make the dependency visible immediately — raise it in the daily scrum and coordinate with the other team (e.g., through a cross-team sync or Scrum of Scrums)
Build a permanent workaround without telling the other team
Escalate to both sponsors before speaking to the other team
questions.exp: Coordinating with other projects and teams starts with transparency and direct collaboration. Cross-team dependency mechanisms (Scrum of Scrums, dependency boards) exist for exactly this; hiding the slip or escalating before collaborating are both anti-patterns.

questions.q: A steering committee asks for 'one number' that says whether the project is on track. What should the PM propose instead?
opts: Report SPI alone, since schedule is most visible
A small balanced set of metrics — schedule and cost performance plus value/outcome and quality indicators — because no single number captures project health
Report percent complete of tasks
Whatever number looks best that month
questions.exp: Developing project metrics means measuring what matters: baseline performance, quality, risk, and progress toward business value. A single index invites gaming and blind spots — a project can have SPI = 1.0 while building something nobody will use.

questions.q: A three-person, six-week internal project is told to produce the PMO's full 40-artifact documentation set. What should the PM do?
opts: Produce all 40 artifacts — compliance is compliance
Tailor: agree with the PMO on the minimal set of artifacts that provides needed governance for a project of this size and risk
Produce no documentation since the project is small
Copy artifacts from a previous project and rename them
questions.exp: 'Identify and tailor needed artifacts' is an explicit enabler of evaluating project status. Artifacts exist to serve decisions and governance — right-sizing them to project complexity is expected practice, negotiated with the PMO rather than ignored.

questions.q: Stakeholders complain they never know sprint status until the monthly report arrives. What is the MOST agile remedy?
opts: Send the monthly report twice a month
Use information radiators — a visible, continuously current board and burndown accessible to everyone at any time
Require stakeholders to email the PM for status
Add stakeholders to the team's private chat channel
questions.exp: Agile favors transparent, always-available information radiators (boards, burndowns, dashboards) over periodic push reporting. This also satisfies the ECO enabler of ensuring artifacts are accessible to those who need them.

questions.q: A project reports CPI = 1.08 and SPI = 0.92. Where should the PM focus corrective action?
opts: Cost — the project is over budget
Schedule — the project is under budget but behind schedule, so recovery options like fast-tracking or selective crashing should be evaluated
Both equally — the indices are both broken
Nowhere — both indices are within 10% of 1.0 so nothing matters
questions.exp: CPI > 1 means cost efficiency is good ($1.08 of value per dollar); SPI < 1 means work is falling behind plan. The cost headroom may even fund schedule recovery (crashing). Reading the two indices together directs attention correctly.

questions.q: Metrics show the project will miss a committed date, and the PM has developed two viable recovery options. The sponsor 'prefers not to alarm the committee.' What should the PM do?
opts: Honor the sponsor's preference and omit the forecast
Report the accurate forecast along with the recovery options and a recommendation
Report the slip but withhold the options to increase urgency
Delay reporting until the slip becomes undeniable
questions.exp: Communicating project status means accurate, decision-useful information: the honest forecast plus analyzed options. Suppressing material information to manage optics fails both the ECO enabler and PMI's honesty standard — bad news early is cheaper than bad news late.

questions.q: A Kanban team's average cycle time has climbed from 4 days to 9 days over two months. What should the team examine FIRST?
opts: Whether team members are working hard enough
Flow data — WIP against limits, blocked items, and where work ages on the board — to find the bottleneck
Whether to switch from Kanban to Scrum
Whether cycle time is worth measuring at all
questions.exp: Measuring, analyzing, and updating metrics means responding to signals with flow analysis: rising cycle time usually traces to WIP creep, a blocked-work pileup, or a bottleneck stage. The system, not individual effort, is where the answer lives.

questions.q: All deliverables are accepted and the team is eager to disband, but two vendor contracts have unresolved invoices and one warranty claim is open. What should the PM do?
opts: Close the project — procurement issues belong to the finance department now
Complete procurement and financial closure — resolve claims, verify final payments, formally close contracts — as part of concluding the project
Close the project and reopen it if the claim escalates
Transfer the open contracts to whichever PM is available
questions.exp: Managing project closure includes concluding procurement and financial activities: settling claims, verifying payments, and formally closing agreements. A project with open contractual obligations is not closed, whatever the deliverable status says.

questions.q: A new system is ready for go-live, but the operations team has had no training, no runbooks exist, and no support model is agreed. The sponsor wants to close the project at go-live. What should the PM do?
opts: Close as requested — operations problems are not project problems
Validate transition readiness first — confirm operational acceptance criteria, training, documentation, and support handoff before closure
Extend the project indefinitely to run the system
Go live and email the runbooks later
questions.exp: 'Validate readiness for transition' is an explicit closure enabler. Value is realized in operations; handing over a system the receiving team cannot run destroys the benefit the project was funded to create. Closure follows a verified handoff.

questions.q: An agile product effort is ending and the long-lived product team will keep evolving the product. What should closure emphasize?
opts: Archiving the backlog and deleting the team's boards
A final retrospective to capture knowledge, confirmation of backlog and product ownership handoff, and celebrating/releasing people respectfully
An exhaustive closure report matching the predictive template
Nothing — agile efforts do not close
questions.exp: Agile closure still fulfills the ECO's closure task, tailored to context: final retrospective and lessons, clean transition of the backlog and product ownership, financial and procurement wrap-up, and deliberate release of the team.

questions.q: Governance cancels a project at 40% complete after a strategy shift. The team wants to walk away immediately. What must the PM do?
opts: Nothing — a cancelled project needs no closure
Perform closure anyway: document the state of work, capture lessons learned, close contracts and financials, archive records, and release resources formally
Keep the team billing until the fiscal year ends
Delete the project records to avoid embarrassment
questions.exp: Cancelled projects require the same disciplined closure as completed ones — determine closure criteria, settle contractual and financial obligations, preserve knowledge, and release resources. Abandonment without closure leaks money, knowledge, and legal exposure.

flashcards.q: 'Plan financial reporting' (2026 ECO) — what should the plan define?
flashcards.a:
For EACH governance audience (sponsor, finance, steering committee):
• Content — actuals, forecast (EAC/ETC), variances vs thresholds, reserve status
• Format and level of detail
• Frequency/cadence
• Escalation triggers tied to variance thresholds

Designed once, upfront, in the cost/financial management plan — different audiences legitimately need different views.

flashcards.q: How do you size a contingency reserve using EMV?
flashcards.a:
Sum the EMV of the identified risks: EMV = Probability × Impact per risk.

Example: 20%×$40K + 50%×$10K + 10%×$100K = 8K + 5K + 10K = $23K contingency.

This links quantitative risk analysis directly to financial planning — a named enabler of the 2026 Plan and Manage Finance task.

flashcards.q: What does 'plan spend tracking throughout the project life cycle' require?
flashcards.a:
Designed-in cost visibility from day one: how actuals are collected, reporting cadence, forecasting method (EAC/burn rate), and variance thresholds that trigger action.

Agile version: stable team cost per iteration makes burn rate simple — runway = remaining funds ÷ cost per sprint.

flashcards.q: A financial variance breaches the governance threshold. What is the PM's required pattern?
flashcards.a:
1. Analyze the cause
2. Update the forecast (EAC/VAC)
3. Develop response options
4. Engage the governance process as the threshold requires

Never: hide it, shuffle budget between accounts to mask it, or redefine the threshold. Thresholds exist to trigger escalation with analysis attached.

flashcards.q: How should a PM 'anticipate future finance challenges' on multi-year projects?
flashcards.a:
Treat inflation, currency movement, funding cycles, and market volatility as plannable financial risks:
• Contract mechanisms (FP-EPA economic price adjustment)
• Currency provisions
• Financial reserves sized for the exposure
• Purchase-timing strategies

Future price movement is a risk to plan for, not a surprise to react to.

flashcards.q: Contingency reserve is burning faster than risk exposure is retiring. What should the PM do?
flashcards.a:
Continuously compare reserve REMAINING vs. risk exposure REMAINING.

If a shortfall is projected: reassess the risk register, requantify exposure, and raise it with the sponsor/governance early — while options still exist.

Managing financial reserves is an active, ongoing duty — not a set-and-forget allocation.

flashcards.q: What is the FIRST step of value-based delivery?
flashcards.a:
Identify value components WITH key stakeholders — what outcomes count as value (revenue, cost savings, risk reduction, compliance, sustainability, customer outcomes) and how each will be measured.

If value is undefined or contested, prioritization and benefit tracking have no foundation.

flashcards.q: How should work be prioritized under value-based delivery?
flashcards.a:
By value evidence, not stakeholder volume:
• Expected business value / benefit
• Cost of delay (higher CoD first, for equal duration)
• Risk reduction and learning value
• Customer feedback and adoption data

The loudest stakeholder is not a prioritization technique.

flashcards.q: What does 'verify a measurement system is in place to track benefits' mean in practice?
flashcards.a:
Before (or as) delivery starts:
• Capture the BASELINE metric now (you can't prove a 20% improvement without the 'before')
• Define the benefit metrics, owner, timing, and reporting
• Confirm the tracking mechanism actually exists

Reconstructing baselines at closure is too late.

flashcards.q: What should happen when a project is on-baseline but its business case has eroded?
flashcards.a:
Reassess the business case with the sponsor and present options: continue, modify, or cancel.

A project can be perfectly on time and on budget and still not be worth finishing. Examining business value THROUGHOUT the project is an explicit 2026 enabler — value is inspected continuously, not assumed from approval.

flashcards.q: Cost of Quality — the four categories with examples
flashcards.a:
Costs of CONFORMANCE:
• Prevention — training, design reviews, better processes
• Appraisal — inspection, testing, audits

Costs of NONCONFORMANCE:
• Internal failure — scrap and rework found BEFORE delivery
• External failure — warranty, recalls, reputational damage AFTER delivery (the most expensive)

flashcards.q: When is extra quality spending justified — and when is it gold plating?
flashcards.a:
Justified: prevention/appraisal investment that reduces TOTAL cost of quality (e.g., $20K of automated checks that avoids $75K of rework and warranty costs). That is CoQ optimization.

Gold plating: adding unrequested features or scope beyond requirements.

Strengthening conformance to requirements is the opposite of gold plating.

flashcards.q: How does sustainability appear inside the 2026 quality task?
flashcards.a:
The ECO pairs 'cost of quality (CoQ) and sustainability' in one enabler. Sustainability commitments (energy efficiency, materials standards, lifecycle impact) become:
• Measurable quality requirements
• Acceptance criteria
• Items verified in ongoing quality reviews

Not aspirations bolted on after delivery.

flashcards.q: The same defect type escapes for several sprints. What is the agile quality response?
flashcards.a:
Treat it as a PROCESS signal, not a person problem:
1. Root cause analysis in the retrospective
2. Update the Definition of Done, automated tests, or engineering practices so the defect class cannot escape again

This is continuous improvement — building quality in, not inspecting it in at the end.

flashcards.q: How is scope managed differently across a hybrid project?
flashcards.a:
Contracted / stable components: scope baseline (scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary) with integrated change control.

Evolving components: prioritized product backlog, reordered by the Product Owner.

Hybrid means applying the RIGHT control to each part — not forcing one model onto everything.

flashcards.q: Control Quality vs. Validate Scope — which comes first and why?
flashcards.a:
Control Quality FIRST: internal inspection confirms the deliverable is correct.

Validate Scope SECOND: the customer/sponsor formally ACCEPTS the verified deliverable.

Memory: verify it's right, then get it signed off. Accepted deliverables then feed project/phase closure.

flashcards.q: How do you forecast a release date from velocity — and what caveat applies?
flashcards.a:
Sprints remaining ≈ remaining story points ÷ average velocity.
Example: 240 points ÷ 30/sprint ≈ 8 sprints.

Caveat: velocity is historical data, so present a RANGE and re-forecast every sprint. It is a forecast, never a guarantee — and never a target management can demand.

flashcards.q: A milestone slips. What is the FIRST analysis step before choosing a response?
flashcards.a:
Check the schedule network: is the slipped work on the CRITICAL PATH, and how does the delay propagate through dependencies?

A slip inside available float changes nothing; the same slip on the critical path moves the end date.

Impact analysis first — then evaluate responses (fast-tracking, crashing, scope options).

flashcards.q: What drives the choice of predictive, agile, or hybrid delivery?
flashcards.a:
An assessment of project context — never preference or fashion:
• Requirements stability and clarity
• Uncertainty and rate of change
• Compliance/contractual constraints
• Stakeholder availability for feedback
• Team capability and organizational culture

Stable + regulated → predictive strengths. Evolving + feedback-rich → agile strengths. Mixed → hybrid, applying each where it fits.

flashcards.q: What must happen at closure — even if the project is cancelled?
flashcards.a:
1. Obtain stakeholder approval / document final state
2. Validate transition readiness (operations trained, runbooks, support model)
3. Close procurements — resolve claims, verify final payments
4. Financial closure and reserve reconciliation
5. Final lessons learned / retrospective
6. Archive records, release resources formally

Cancelled projects get the SAME disciplined closure — abandonment leaks money, knowledge, and legal exposure.
